title: Beyond education for economic productivity alone: The Capabilities Approach - ScienceDirect
excerpts:
Section Title: Beyond education for economic productivity alone: The Capabilities Approach > Introduction
Content:
Speaking at the 2011 Hal Wootten Lecture in Australia, Nussbaum also argues for the importance of the humanities in developing complete citizens who can think for themselves, criticise tradition and authority, and understand the significance of another person's sufferings and achievements. She considers the humanities necessary to keep democracy alive as they provide an important space in which to develop critical capabilities for individual and societal flourishing, which education practice has a duty to include in the curriculum in balance with the sciences Nussbaum, 1997, Nussbaum, 2011.

title: Martha Nussbaum on Living (and Eating) Morally
excerpts: Martha Nussbaum is a philosopher and the Ernst Freund Distinguished Service Professor of Law and Ethics at the University of Chicago.

title: The Capability Approach (Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy)
excerpts:
Section Title: The Capability Approach > ... > 1.1 Background: Critique of other accounts
Content:
Although we can trace some aspects of the capability approach back to,
among others, Aristotle, Adam Smith, and Karl Marx (see Nussbaum 1988,
1992, 2020; Sen 1993a, 1999: 14, 24; Walsh 2000 and 2003), it is
economist-philosopher Amartya Sen who pioneered the approach and
philosopher Martha Nussbaum and a growing number of other scholars
across the humanities and the social sciences who have significantly
developed it. The capability approach purports that freedom to achieve
well-being is a matter of what people are able to do and to be, and
thus the kind of life they are effectively able to lead.
